questions.question: A firm has Q = LK. To produce Q = 100 while using as little capital K as possible subject to using exactly L = 20 labor-hours, what K is required and what does this point represent?

choices.A: K = 5; it is one point on the Q = 100 isoquant.
choices.B: K = 20; equal inputs are always required on an isoquant.
choices.D: K = 0; labor alone can produce output.

questions.explanation: With Q=LK=100 and L=20, K=5. This is one input combination on the isoquant for output 100. B assumes symmetry means equality. C confuses output with an input. D ignores the multiplicative technology.

questions.question: For Q = min{2L, K}, what is the cost-minimizing input bundle on the Q = 40 isoquant if labor costs $5 and capital costs $1?

choices.A: L = 20, K = 40 because the fixed-proportions kink satisfies 2L = K = 40.
choices.B: L = 0, K = 40 because capital is cheaper.
choices.C: L = 20, K = 20 because equal input quantities minimize cost.
choices.D: L = 40, K = 0 because labor creates the first term.

questions.explanation: To produce 40, both 2L and K must be at least 40, so L=20 and K=40. B and D ignore the complementary input requirement. C produces only min(40,20)=20.

questions.question: For Q = 6sqrt(LK), the firm moves from the Q = 60 isoquant to the Q = 120 isoquant while keeping the input ratio L/K constant. What happens to required L and K?

choices.A: Both inputs double because the technology has constant returns to scale.
choices.B: Both inputs quadruple because output doubles.
choices.C: Only labor doubles because isoquants measure labor changes.
choices.D: Inputs are unchanged because the input ratio is unchanged.

questions.explanation: The function is homogeneous of degree 1, so doubling output at a constant input ratio requires doubling both inputs. B confuses output doubling with area scaling. C ignores capital. D confuses unchanged ratio with unchanged quantities.

questions.question: For Q = L + 2K, how does the isoquant for Q = 80 compare with the isoquant for Q = 40?

choices.A: It is parallel and farther from the origin, with intercepts twice as large.
choices.B: It is steeper because higher output requires more capital.
choices.C: It crosses the Q = 40 isoquant at L = 40, K = 20.
choices.D: It is closer to the origin because higher output is more efficient.

questions.explanation: Isoquants are L+2K=Q, so slope is always -1/2. Q=80 has L-intercept 80 and K-intercept 40, twice those of Q=40. B incorrectly changes the slope. C violates non-intersection. D reverses output ranking.

questions.question: Well-behaved isoquants for Q = L^(1/2)K^(1/2) are described as convex to the origin. What does that curvature mean?

choices.A: As the firm uses more labor and less capital, labor becomes less able to substitute for capital at the margin.
choices.B: Labor and capital are perfect substitutes with a constant slope.
choices.C: The firm must use labor and capital in a fixed one-to-one ratio.
choices.D: Higher isoquants eventually intersect lower isoquants.

questions.explanation: Convex isoquants reflect diminishing technical substitutability: as labor becomes abundant relative to capital, extra labor replaces less capital. B describes linear isoquants. C describes right-angle isoquants. D violates production-function consistency.

questions.question: In an isoquant diagram with labor on the horizontal axis and capital on the vertical axis, what does a flatter point on the same isoquant indicate?

choices.A: The firm is using relatively more labor and less capital, so the marginal product of labor is lower relative to capital.
choices.B: The firm is producing less output because the curve is flatter.
choices.C: The firm has lower total cost because the slope is closer to zero.
choices.D: The firm has moved to a higher isoquant.

questions.explanation: The isoquant slope is -MPL/MPK. A flatter slope means MPL is lower relative to MPK, usually where labor is abundant. B confuses slope with output level. C requires input prices. D confuses movement along an isoquant with shifting isoquants.

questions.question: Why can two isoquants from the same production function not cross?

choices.A: Because one input bundle cannot produce two different maximum output levels under the same technology.
choices.C: Because marginal products must be negative at a crossing.
choices.D: Because all technologies have constant returns to scale.

questions.explanation: If two isoquants crossed, the same input bundle would correspond to two different output levels, contradicting the production function. B adds cost minimization unnecessarily. C is not implied. D is false.

questions.question: A hospital can perform 200 scans per day using several combinations of technicians and MRI machine-hours. Administrators want to list all combinations that achieve exactly 200 scans before considering wages or machine rental rates. Which tool should they use?

choices.B: A demand curve for MRI scans.
choices.C: An isocost line using technician wages.

questions.explanation: An isoquant collects all input bundles producing a given output. B is about consumers. C requires prices and cost, which the problem says to ignore initially. D concerns revenue, not input combinations.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes an isoquant from an indifference curve?

choices.A: An isoquant holds output constant across input bundles; an indifference curve holds utility constant across consumption bundles.
choices.B: An isoquant must be upward sloping; an indifference curve must be downward sloping.
choices.C: An isoquant includes input prices; an indifference curve includes consumer income.
choices.D: An isoquant applies only to fixed-proportions technologies; an indifference curve applies only to Cobb-Douglas preferences.

questions.explanation: Isoquants represent equal output from input bundles; indifference curves represent equal utility from consumption bundles. B is wrong because typical isoquants are downward sloping. C confuses constraints with curves. D is far too narrow.

questions.question: A firm initially uses technology Q = L + K. After automation, technology becomes Q = 2L + K. For the Q = 100 isoquant, what changes? Welfare analysis is not directly applicable here, so this substitutes a second Type 2 question.

choices.A: The labor intercept falls from 100 to 50, and the isoquant becomes steeper because labor is more productive.
choices.B: The capital intercept falls from 100 to 50 because capital is more productive.
choices.C: The isoquant shifts outward because better technology requires more inputs.
choices.D: The slope stays -1 because output is still linear.

questions.explanation: Before automation, Q=100 has L-intercept 100 and slope -1. After automation, 2L+K=100 has L-intercept 50 and slope -2. Labor productivity rises, so less labor is needed if using only labor. B changes the wrong intercept. C reverses the effect of better technology. D ignores the changed marginal product ratio.
